key_observations: Strong 8-week progression: from 1 run/9.5km weekly to 2-3 runs/13-19km. Training volume increased 103% over last 4 weeks with structured frequency.
Heart rate creeping upward (158→164 bpm avg) while pace improves (6:11→5:59 min/km), suggesting cardiovascular adaptation and anaerobic threshold development.
Two high-intensity sessions (Jul 2: 167.8 avg HR; Jul 6: 175.7 avg HR) indicate deliberate intensity work, but limited recovery week—last easy run (Jul 5) was only moderately easy at 147.7 bpm.
warnings: Acute-chronic ratio of 1.18 is elevated; last 2 weeks show sustained high load without a proper recovery week. Risk of overtraining if intensity doesn't ease.
Gap in data (Jun 8–Jun 22): 2-week blackout makes trend continuity unclear. Possible missed workouts or tracking issue.
Football session (Jun 27) was high-HR activity (158 avg, 188 max) but included in running metrics—cross-training load not isolated in analysis.
recommendations: Implement a recovery week: reduce to 1–2 easy runs (145–150 bpm target) for 7 days to consolidate gains and lower fatigue markers before next build phase.
Maintain current frequency (3 runs/week) but alternate hard/easy structure: one tempo/threshold session, one easy, one moderate. Current runs lack clear intensity demarcation.
Invest in HR monitor or wearable with training load metrics (Training Stress Score, Recovery Status) to prevent overreach—missing HR data on early runs limits early fatigue detection.

weeks_analysis.assessment: Base-building phase: single low-frequency run, steady pace (6:11). No HR data limits fitness assessment, but volume adequate for maintenance.
Slight step-back in volume; pace consistent (6:12). Suggests recovery week or reduced availability. Single run insufficient for adaptation stimulus.
Lowest volume in dataset; pace improves to 5:47. Single run indicates minimal training stress. No HR data prevents aerobic efficiency evaluation.
Structural shift: 3 runs, volume +149%. HR data appears; avg 158 bpm suggests mostly Z2–Z3 work. Training frequency now adequate; fatigue emerging.
Peak load week: 3 runs, +23% volume increase, 160 bpm avg. Includes football (cross-training). HR creep (+2 bpm) and sustained 19+ km signals adaptation but fatigue risk.
Intended taper (-30% km) but HR rises to 164 bpm—cardiovascular intensity preserved despite volume reduction. Jul 6 run (175.7 bpm) suggests hard session instead of rest. Incomplete recovery.
